completeness.rationale: Extracted most expected information: ~5,000 tasks, slow on description search vs fast on title search, v2.2 to v2.3 regression, ~2 weeks ago, 10-15 second delays, long descriptions. Missed CPU spike detail, but that's a system-level observation the reporter didn't volunteer and the agent didn't ask about.

accuracy.rationale: The root cause hypothesis is spot-on: 'a change from indexed search to naive string scanning' is essentially exactly what happened (FTS5 index to LIKE query). The summary correctly identifies it as a v2.3 regression, correctly distinguishes title vs description search behavior, and all facts match the ground truth.

efficiency.rationale: Only 2 agent questions to reach a solid triage — very efficient. The browser question was slightly wasted (it's a desktop app, not browser-based), but the agent corrected course gracefully. Could have asked about CPU/memory behavior to get the CPU spike detail, but the conversation was lean overall.

question_quality.rationale: First question effectively separated search type, location, and dataset size — drawing out the critical title-vs-description distinction. Second question targeted environment and version, which directly revealed the v2.3 regression. The browser sub-question was a minor miss for a desktop app, but the overall diagnostic reasoning was strong.

actionability.rationale: The summary is immediately actionable: it tells a developer exactly where to look (diff search implementation between v2.2 and v2.3, check description indexing), provides clear reproduction steps with specific data requirements, and even proposes a performance test. A developer could start investigating right away.

notable_strengths: Excellent root cause hypothesis that closely matches the actual cause — identified indexing removal as a likely culprit
Very efficient: only 2 turns to extract nearly all critical information
Reproduction steps are specific and well-structured, including the differential test (title vs description)
Summary is well-organized with clear separation of problem, hypothesis, reproduction, and recommended fix
notable_weaknesses: Asked about browser version for what turned out to be a desktop application
Did not ask about system resource behavior (CPU/memory) during slow searches, missing the CPU spike signal
Did not ask whether the reporter had tried any workarounds or diagnostics (e.g., reindexing, cache clearing)
most_insightful_question: What are you searching for (e.g., a keyword, a tag, a filter combination)? ... Roughly how many tasks are in your workspace? — This compound question efficiently drew out the critical title-vs-description distinction and dataset size in a single turn.
missed_opportunities: Asking about CPU or memory usage during slow searches would have revealed the 100% single-core CPU spike, further confirming sequential scanning
Asking whether the reporter had tried clearing cache or reindexing could have provided additional diagnostic signal and a potential workaround
